content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 5, 1999


Frederick P. Kovol
Solvang City Council
1676 Nordentoft Way
Solvang, California 93463-2115

Re: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-99-106

Dear Mr. Kovol:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any conduct in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place.

QUESTION

May you participate in decisionmaking on the appeal in City of Solvang and Frederick P. Kovol v. Santa Ynez River Water Conservation District, Improvement District No.1, after the Respondents in that appeal have filed a motion seeking sanctions from you in your capacity as an individual plaintiff/appellant?

CONCLUSION

     Yes. We conclude that, having reported no other potentially disqualifying financial interest in the appeal, you will not violate the Act's conflict of interest provisions by making or participating in governmental decisions on this appeal. It is not "foreseeable," within the meaning of the Act, that you will experience any personal financial effect from the appeal simply because the Respondents have filed a motion alleging that the appeal is "frivolous." A material financial effect is foreseeable if the Court of Appeal notifies the parties under Appellate Rule 26(e) that the court is considering the imposition of sanctions. But until that time, you may participate in governmental decisions relating to litigation of this appeal.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-99-106
Page 2

FACTS

On August 25, 1995, the City of Solvang (the "city") filed a lawsuit against the Santa
Ynez River Water Conservation District, Improvement District No.1 (the "water district") for
ballot fraud, misrepresentation, and violation of the constitutional debt limit. The city's special
ballot counsel, Nossaman, Guthner et al., had indicated that, for standing purposes, it was necessary
that a ratepayer be named as a plaintiff in addition to the City. Special counsel opined that it
would be best to have a councilmember serve in this capacity. A councilmember, Mayor
William Warwick, was selected for this role. On August 28, 1995, the city council resolved
"that it is in the City's best interests, and that a direct and substantial public purpose benefiting
the City will occur from supporting William Warwick's taxpayer/ratepayer action against [the
water district]...". (Resolution 95-378).

Among other things, Resolution 95-378 found that Mr. Warwick had standing to sue
under CCP Section 526a — an advantage that the city did not have; that the city was the real
party interested in the outcome of the ratepayer action; that the interests of the ratepayer and the
city in this action were "harmonious and identical;" and that it would be economically and
legally advantageous that the ratepayer and the city be joint plaintiffs and represented by the
same special counsel. The city accordingly promised, without reservations, to pay all reasonable
attorneys' fees and costs incurred by Mr. Warwick in the action.

In 1996, you were elected to the Solvang City Council. Mr. Warwick lost his seat in the
same election. However, for reasons of expediency, Mr. Warwick was left on the lawsuit as the
named ratepayer. On December 5, 1997, a judgment was entered on behalf of the water district. In
January 1998, the city council voted to appeal. Mr. Warwick then withdrew from the action. The
court permitted a substitution of parties so that certain causes of action could be appealed as to all
parties. You were named as the ratepayer/plaintiff on appeal, replacing Mr. Warwick in the
litigation. The action is now entitled City of Solvang and Frederick P. Kovol v. Santa Ynez River
Water Conservation District, Improvement District No.1.

On January 13, 1999, the water district filed a motion for sanctions against the city, against
you in your capacity as the named ratepayer, and against the appellate attorneys, Sheppard, Mullen
et al., on the ground that the appeal was "frivolous." The water district asked for monetary sanctions
against only you and the law firm.

The city filed an opposition to the motion for sanctions, arguing that sanctions are improper
"on the merits" and, further, that the water district is attempting to chill the relationship between the
city and you, the city's joint plaintiff. Because sanctions are being sought against you in your
capacity as an individual plaintiff, you have been advised by the city attorney that you might have
a conflict of interest in any further city council decisions relating to this appeal. You have therefore
refrained from participation in closed session meetings or communications relating to the appeal,
either as council member or ratepayer/plaintiff.

File No. A-99-106
Page 3

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions are designed to ensure that public officials perform
their duties impartially, free from bias attributable to their own financial interests or those of persons
who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public
official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

There is no doubt that you are a "public official" within the meaning of the Act,² and your
inquiry presupposes that, if permissible, you will be making, or participating in making,
governmental decisions relating to the appeal.³ A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the official's economic interests, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) To decide whether you have a financial
interest in any decision relating to the appeal, we must first establish what constitutes a "material
financial effect," and then whether any such effect is "reasonably foreseeable" under the
circumstances prevailing at the time of the decision.⁴

You have not reported any economic interest in the appeal (apart from the interest common
to all ratepayers within the jurisdiction) except for your potential liability for the monetary sanctions
sought by the water district. Rather, your inquiry is focused on the possibility that the motion for
sanctions might create a conflict of interest where none had existed before. The only economic
interest at issue is thus the possible personal financial effect that this motion might have on you.

A governmental decision has a "personal financial effect" on a public official if the decision
will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official, or his or her
immediate family, materially increasing or decreasing. (Regulation 18703.5.) Such an effect is
material if it amounts to $250 or more in any twelve month period. (Regulation 18705.5).

Your question reduces down to this: is it reasonably foreseeable that the court might
ultimately award sanctions against you in the amount of $250 or more? If the answer is "no," you
will not have a financial interest in decisions relating to the conduct of the appeal.

___________________________

² A "public official" is defined as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency ...." (Section 82048.). You note that you are acting as a member of the public in your role as
"ratepayer plaintiff." However, our analysis is directed to your actions as a councilmember, and the financial effect
of concern is not the general effect of the lawsuit on ratepayers, but the specific effect of monetary sanctions on you.

³ Commission regulations describe in detail what constitutes making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision. (Regulations 18702.1, 18702.2, and 18702.3, respectively.)

⁴ The effect of a decision is "reasonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

File No. A-99-106
Page 4

It is not clear that you yourself would be obligated to pay any sanctions the court might award, since the city might be required (or might choose) to pay the impost on your behalf. However, we need not speculate on who might ultimately pay an award of sanctions if the award itself is not foreseeable. We do not regard the filing of this motion as sufficient to make the ultimate award of sanctions "foreseeable," within the meaning of the Act. The Commission is not in a position to forecast how the Court of Appeal will dispose of the motion and, indeed, the Commission cannot know whether the court will consider the imposition of sanctions at all.

Appellate Rule 26(e) was recently amended to reduce the burden of defending against motions alleging that an appeal is frivolous. Under the rule now in effect, the Respondent is required to file a separate motion for sanctions, but the Appellant is not required to respond, unless and until the Court of Appeal provides written notice that it is considering the imposition of sanctions. Until the court signals that it regards the matter to be worthy of response, we cannot say that sanctions are "foreseeable" within the meaning of the Act.

As a practical matter, appeals are labeled "frivolous" by respondents more often than they are by the courts. The motion for sanctions is normally made in the early briefing stages of the appellate process, but not resolved until the appeal has been decided on its merits. If we were to conclude that public officials involved in litigation could acquire a disqualifying financial interest in an appeal whenever an opponent claims that the appeal is "frivolous," we would set the stage for tactical maneuvers unrelated to the potential for genuine conflicts of interest. Rule 26(e) requires that the court specifically identify that subset of appeals that may be subject to sanctions as "frivolous," and we conclude that an award of sanctions for a frivolous appeal is "foreseeable" under the Act only after the court has notified the appellant that the court is considering such sanctions.

If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
